text.title: Status Reporting: RAG Policy, Action Items, Antipatterns
text.summary: Rules for producing honest, metric-grounded status reports and extracting actionable meeting outputs.

rule.statement: The status headline must reflect the worst single metric, not the average. If schedule is RED and cost is GREEN, overall status is RED.
rule.rationale: Averaging metrics hides the failing dimension. Sponsors need to know the worst, not the mean — they will discover it themselves and the trust damage is compounded by the delay.

rule.statement: Status reports must cite metric values or commit/issue IDs as evidence. Reject "good progress, all on track" without quantitative support.
rule.rationale: LLMs and human PMs both default to optimism. Grounding on metrics or artefact IDs is the only defence against false green status.

rule.statement: Send status reports on a fixed day and time every week. Predictability makes the ritual; irregular cadence erodes trust.
rule.rationale: Stakeholders who cannot predict when the next report arrives start asking for ad-hoc updates, which costs more than the regular report.

section.title: Meeting Output

rule.statement: Every meeting must produce a decisions log and an action-item list. Action items must have: text, owner, and due date — or they are not action items.
rule.rationale: Action items without owner and due date pile up as orphans. The list becomes a backlog no one owns and nothing gets done.

rule.statement: Document decision rationale, not just the decision. "We chose Y because Z given trade-off with X" is a decision record. "We chose Y" is not.
rule.rationale: Decisions without rationale get re-litigated when context changes. Preserved dissent and trade-off reasoning prevent re-opening settled questions.

section.reference: Extracts action items with owner and due date from markdown meeting notes

antipattern.description: Status report with no risks listed when the risk register is non-empty.
antipattern.reason: Empty risk section signals either the PM is not monitoring or is suppressing bad news. Require at least the top-3 risks by impact.

antipattern.description: Status reports sent at "9 AM" without timezone, distributed across multiple timezones.
antipattern.reason: Recipients in different timezones receive conflicting effective send times; pin UTC plus local timezone in all scheduled communications.

antipattern.description: Escalation contact not reachable; no backup defined.
antipattern.reason: Escalation paths fail precisely when the primary is on leave or in a different incident. Always define primary + backup + out-of-office fallback.
